text: hello friends this video demonstrates laparoscopic management of intestinal obstruction due to band additions a thirty eight year old male presented to emerging with the features of acute small bowel obstruction and he had a history of open appendectomy twenty years back and a left left segmental colectomy for colonic lipoma eight years back in another center following which he developed intermittent features of cervical intestinal obstruction which was managed conservatively elsewhere on admission his vitals were stable abdominal distension was there but there is no suggestion of peritonitis. So we did a CT scan and there is a suggestion of proximal small bowel obstruction, maybe because of intestinal postoperative band adhesions. These patients can be managed laparoscopically, but proper case selection is the key. So there are certain prerequisites like proximal small bowel obstruction, early obstruction, recurrent small intestinal obstruction, suspected band in a hemodynamically stable patient can be chosen for the laparoscopy. Proper history tracking of previous surgery and post-operative course, adequate resuscitation with nasogastric decompression and other planning port position according to previous operative scars is very important.

Initial port position should be preferably Hasson's open technique. But if you are confident that upper abdominal axis can begin, one try of Vires can be done. But never hesitate to convert. So keep an open trolley ready. Whenever you feel it should be converted, you can convert to open. so let's have a look at CT scan the CCT of whole abdomen it's showing dilated jejunal loops significant dilated jejunal loops and air fluid level you can see clear features of small bowel obstruction and but there is wall enhancement so bowels are apparently healthy and there is there you can be able to see a small sudden zone of transition over there that is a site of obstruction most operatively

The surgeon begins the procedure with a 5mm port in the epigastric region, observing dilated proximal bowel loops and collapsed distal ileal loops. Adhesions of the small bowel to the anterior abdominal wall are visible, likely near a previous operative scar. The surgeon carefully performs adhesiolysis using scissors and atraumatic graspers, gently pulling the bowel downward to reveal the dissection plane. The peritoneum is dissected along with the bowel to prevent injury, with minor bleeding observed that is expected to self-resolve.

The surgeon completes adhesiolysis on one loop of the bowel, revealing another loop adhered to a previous operative scar. The adjacent peritoneum is carefully dissected away from the bowel surface to avoid injury. After checking for hemostasis and any inadvertent tears, the surgeon identifies a thick fibrous band holding the anti-mesenteric border of the terminal ileal loop. The band, minimally vascular, extends underneath and connects to another loop of bowel. The surgeon cautiously divides the band using a harmonic shear.

After dividing the fibrous band, the surgeon begins tracing the bowel from the ileocecal junction, observing the collapsed loops of bowel. Using two atraumatic bowel graspers, the surgeon gently pulls the bowel, noting healthy bowel loops and visible peristalsis. The previously obscured band is now visible, connecting the distal antimesentery border of the jejunum with the antimesentery border of the terminal ileum, which was causing the obstruction.

The surgeon traces the proximal dilated bowel loops, ensuring that only adhesions causing or potentially causing obstruction are addressed. The video shows the port sites from previous surgeries, including appendectomy and segmental colectomy.
